title: Motion of Charged Particle in Magnetic Field

concepts.title: Motion of Charge in Magnetic Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Motion of Charge in Magnetic Field 

When a charge is moving a magnetic field, then the force acting on that charge is given by

This is called Lorentz force . Here. $\overrightarrow{{{F}_{m}}}$ is the magnetic force on a charge q moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$.

Following points are worth noting regarding the above expressions.

 

(i) The magnitude of $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}$ is, where $\theta $ is the angle between $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$.

(ii) $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}$ is zero when,

(a) $\text{B}=0$, i.e., no magnetic field is present.

(b) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }q=0$, i.e., particle is neutral.

(c) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ v}=0$, i.e., charged particle is at rest or

(d) $\theta =0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ or 180, i.e., $\vec{v}\uparrow \uparrow \vec{B}$ or $\vec{v}\uparrow \downarrow \vec{B}$

(iii) $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}$ is maximum at $\theta =90$ and this maximum value is Bqv.

(iv) The units of $\vec{B}$ must be the same as the units of F/ qv. Therefore, the SI unit of $\vec{B}$ is equivalent to $\frac{\text{N}-\text{s}}{\text{C}-\text{m}}$. This unit is called the tesla (abbreviated as T), in honor of Nikola Tesla, the prominent Serbian-American scientist and inventor. Thus,

The CGS unit of $\vec{B}$, the gauss is also in common use.

(v) The formula is true for both uniform and non-uniform magnetic field.

(vi) In equation number (i) $q$ is to be substituted with sign. If $q$ is positive magnetic force is along $\vec{v}\times \vec{B}$ and if $\text{q}$ is negative magnetic force is in a direction opposite to $\vec{v}\times \vec{B}$.

concepts.title: Direction of Force on Charge
concepts.conceptBody:
Direction of Force on Charge 

From the property of cross product we can infer that $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}$ is perpendicular to both $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ or it is perpendicular to the plane formed by $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$. The exact direction of $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}$ can be given by any of the following methods:

(a) Direction of $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}=$ (sign of q) (direction of $\vec{v}\times \vec{B}$) or, as we stated earlier also,

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{m}}} \uparrow \uparrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$ if $\mathrm{q}$ is positive and

 

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{m}}} \uparrow \downarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$ if $\mathrm{q}$ is negative.

(b) Fleming's left hand rule : According to this rule, the forefinger, the central finger and the thumb of the left hand are stretched in such a way that they are mutually perpendicular to each other. If the central finger shows the direction of velocity of positive charge (and forefinger shows the direction of magnetic field $\left( {\vec{B}} \right)$, then the thumb will give the direction of magnetic force $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}$. If instead of positive charge we have the negative charge, then is in opposite direction.

 

(c) Right hand rule : Wrap the fingers of your right hand around the line perpendicular to the plane of $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ as shown in figure, so that they curl around with the sense of rotation from $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ through the smaller angle between them. Your thumb then points in the direction of the force $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}$ on a positive charge. (Alternatively, the direction of the force $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}$ on a positive charge is the direction in which a right-hand thread screw would advance if turned the same way). 
 

 

$\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}\bot \vec{v}$ or $\frac{\overrightarrow{\text{ds}}}{\text{dt}}$. Therefore, $\overrightarrow{{{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}}\bot \overrightarrow{\text{ds}}$ or the work done by the magnetic force in a static magnetic field is zero.

So, from work energy theorem KE and hence the speed of the charged particle remains constant in magnetic field. The magnetic force can change the direction only. It can not increase or decrease the speed or kinetic energy of the particle.

 

Note: By convention the direction of magnetic field B perpendicular to the paper going inwards is shown by  and the direction perpendicular to the paper coming out is shown by $\odot $

Illustration: 

Can a magnet set a resting electron into motion? 

Solution: 

 

Yes. For example, the charge is at rest, but the magnet is moved as shown in the figure, then the charge will be set into motion. this is because in this situation relative to the magnet, the charge is moving with a velocity $\text{v}$ and hence it will experience a force perpendicular to the plane containing the field and motion of magnet.

Note: Actually, in this situation the field is not steady, and a time varying magnetic field produces an electric field due to which charge experiences a force and sets in motion.

concepts.title: Path of Charge Particle in Uniform Magnetic Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Path of a Charged Particle in Uniform Magnetic Field 

The path of a charged particle in uniform magnetic field depends on the angle $\theta $ (the angle between $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ ). Depending on the different values of $\theta $, following three cases are possible.

Case 1. When $\theta $ is 0 or $180^{\circ}$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{m}}}=0$, when $\theta $ is either 0 or 180. Hence, path of the charged particle is a straight line (undeviated) when it enters parallel or antiparallel to magnetic field.

Case 2. When $\theta=90^{\circ}$

When $\theta =90$, the magnetic force is ${{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}=\text{Bqv sin}90=\text{Bqv}$. This magnetic force is perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. Hence, path is a circle. The necessary centripetal force is provided by the magnetic force. Hence, if $\text{r}$ be the radius of the circle, then

$\frac{m v^2}{r}=B q v$ or $r=\frac{\text{mv}}{\text{Bq}}$

This expression of $\text{r}$ can be written in following different ways :

Here, $\text{ P}=$ momentum of particle

$\text{K}=\text{K}.\text{E}. \text{ of } \text{ particle } =\frac{{{\text{P}}^{2}}}{2 \text{ m}} \text{ or } \text{ P}=\sqrt{2\text{Km}}$

We also know that if the charged particle is accelerated by a potential difference of $\text{V}$ volts, it acquires a $\text{KE}$ given by,

$\text{K}=\text{qV}$

Further, time period of the circular path will be

$\text{T}=\frac{2\pi \text{r}}{\text{v}}=\frac{2\pi \left( \frac{\text{mv}}{\text{Bq}} \right)}{\text{v}}=\frac{2\pi \text{m}}{\text{Bq}}$

Or $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{Bq}}$

Or the angular speed $\left( \omega \right)$ of the particle is, $\omega =\frac{2\pi }{\text{T}}=\frac{\text{Bq}}{\text{m}}$

$\therefore$ Frequency of rotation is, $\text{f}=\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}$

Following points are worth noting regarding a circular path :

 

(i) $\text{T},\text{f}$ and $\omega $ are independent of $\text{v}$ while the radius is directly proportional to $\text{v}$. 

(ii) Hence, if two, charged particles of equal mass and charge enter in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ with different speeds ${{\text{v}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{v}}_{2}}\left( >{{\text{v}}_{1}} \right)$ at right angles, then

But ${{\text{T}}_{1}}={{\text{T}}_{2}}$

as shown in figure. ${{\text{r}}_{2}}>{{\text{r}}_{1}}$

Example 1 

A charged particle $q$ enters a magnetic field region which spreads over a length $d$ with a velocity v. Find the angular and sideways deflection and discuss various cases. 

Solution: 

 

$\begin{matrix} \left( r=\frac{mv}{qB} \right) \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Now }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{sin}\theta \right)=\left( \frac{d}{r} \right)=\left( \frac{qBd}{mv} \right) \\ \therefore \theta =\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{qBd}{mv} \right) \\ \end{matrix}$

Side ways deflection, $\text{PR}=\text{r}-\text{rcos}\theta $

$=r\left( 1-\text{cos}\theta \right)\text{ where }\text{ sin}\theta =\left( \frac{qBd}{mv} \right)$

Cases: 

(i) When $(\text{d}>\text{r})$, then deviation is $\pi $.

(ii) when , then deviation is $\pi /2$.

concepts.title: Helical Path of Charge Particle in Magnetic Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Helical Path of Charge Particle in Magnetic Field 

When $\vec{v}$ makes certain angle with the magnetic field. 

  • In this case the motion executed by the charged particle is helical motion.
  • In this case, resolve the velocity vector into two components, one parallel to $\vec{B}$ field and second perpendicular to the magnetic field.

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & {{\text{V}}_{\bot }}=\text{vsin}\theta \\ {} & {{\text{V}}_{\parallel }}=\text{vcos}\theta \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ radius }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ helix }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ {} & =\frac{m{{v}_{\bot }}}{\text{qB}}=\left( \frac{\text{mvsin}\theta }{\text{qB}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

Time period of 1 complete rotation $=\left( \frac{2\pi m}{qB} \right)$

Pitch of the helix = axial distance covered during one complete rotation

Few important points to note: 

  1. The axis of the helix is always parallel to $\vec{B}$ field.
  2. The plane of circular motion is perpendicular to the magnetic field lines.
  3. Pitch of the helix $=\left( \text{vcos}\theta \right)\text{T}$
  4. A line drawn parallel to axis from the initial point of projection of the particle is tangent to the helix and after making every full rotation, the particle will touch this tangent again and again.

If the tangent is taken along the x-axis, then the particle will hit the axis after every pitch. i.e. it will touch the $\text{x}$-axis at a distance.

 

 

Example

An electron gun emits electrons each with K.E. = $2\text{KeV}$ and directed along positive $\text{x}$-axis. All electrons are required to hit the spot $\text{S}$, where $\text{l}=\text{GS}=0.1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}$. A uniform magnetic field exists along GS. 

Find the minimum valve of $B$ required. 

Solution: 

 

Let the initial velocity $\text{v}$ be directed along $\text{x}$-axis,

{This is the velocity parallel to GS}

${{\text{V}}_{\bot }}=\text{vsin}\theta =\text{vsin}60$

{This velocity is perpendicular to GS}

The GS is the tangent line, along with the particle will be touching again and again after every complete rotation.

n (pitch of helix) = GS

⇒ n (v cos 60 T) = l

And $\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{2(\mathrm{~K} \cdot \mathrm{R} .)}{\mathrm{m}}}-\sqrt{\frac{4000 \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}}} \mathrm{J}$, $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{eB}}$

؞ $\mathrm{n}\left[\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2} \times\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{eB}}\right)\right]=1$

؞ $\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \pi \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{el}} \sqrt{\frac{4000 \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}}}$

؞ For minimum value of B, n = 1

؞ $\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mathrm{mm}}{\mathrm{el}} \sqrt{\frac{4000 \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}}}$

concepts.title: Motion of Charge in Combined Magnetic and Electric Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Motion of the charged particle in combined field $(\mathbf{\vec{E}}$ and $\mathbf{\vec{B}})$ 

 

Case I. When $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ and $\vec{v}$ are mutually perpendicular. 

If the particle moves without deflection, then, $\left( q\text{E}=qvB \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

 

Case II. When $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ are parallel to each other 

${{\text{v}}_{\text{x}}}=\left( {{\text{v}}_{0}}\text{cos}\alpha \right)+\left( \frac{\text{qE}}{\text{m}} \right)\text{t}$

But the $\left( {{\text{v}}_{0}}\text{sin}\alpha \right)$ component remains same.

Now, after any time $\text{t}$

${{\text{v}}_{\text{y}}}=\left( {{\text{v}}_{0}}\text{sin}\alpha \right)\text{sin}\theta =\left( {{\text{v}}_{0}}\text{sin}\alpha \right)\text{sin}\left( \omega \text{t} \right)$

$=\left( {{\text{v}}_{0}}\text{sin}\alpha \right)\text{sin}\left( \frac{\text{qBt}}{\text{m}} \right)$

${{\text{v}}_{\text{z}}}=\left( {{\text{v}}_{0}}\text{sin}\alpha \right)\text{cos}\theta $

$=\left( {{\text{v}}_{0}}\text{sin}\alpha \right)\text{cos}\omega \text{t}=\left( {{\text{v}}_{0}}\text{sin}\alpha \right)\text{cos}\left( \frac{\text{qBt}}{\text{m}} \right)$

Radius of curvature of the trajectory $=\frac{{{\text{v}}^{3}}}{\left| \vec{a}\times \vec{v} \right|}$

Where a is the centripetal acceleration.

Note: This is again a type of helical motion but the pitch is not constant.

Case III. Electric and Magnetic field are mutually perpendicular but $\vec{v}$ is not: 

This is a general case when we write the Lorentz force equation.

$\vec{F}=\left[ \text{q}\vec{E}+\text{q}\left( \vec{v}\times \vec{B} \right) \right]$

Assume the velocity at any instant as $\vec{V}={{v}_{x}}\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+{{v}_{y}}\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,+{{v}_{z}}\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\,$ and then write the individual force equation.

$\therefore \left[ \text{m}\left( \frac{\text{d}{{\text{v}}_{\text{x}}}}{\text{dt}} \right)={{\text{F}}_{\text{x}}} \right];\left[ \text{m}\frac{\text{d}{{\text{v}}_{\text{y}}}}{\text{dt}}={{\text{F}}_{\text{y}}} \right];\left[ \text{m}\frac{\text{d}{{\text{v}}_{\text{z}}}}{\text{dt}}={{\text{F}}_{\text{z}}} \right]$
